title: Reading Sensor Data
description: Understand what moisture, temperature, light, and battery readings mean for your plants.

sections.body: Your Plantgotchi sensor measures four metrics. Here's what each one means and how to interpret it.

sections.heading: Moisture (0–100%)
sections.body:
Soil moisture is measured as a percentage. A reading of 0% means bone dry; 100% means completely saturated (which usually means you've overwatered or the pot has no drainage).

  • Below 30% — Most plants will be stressed. Time to water!
  • 30–80% — The default happy range for common houseplants.
  • Above 80% — Risk of root rot for most species. Let the soil dry out.

Succulents and cacti prefer the lower end (20–40%), while tropical plants like ferns may be happy up to 80–90%. Adjust your plant's thresholds accordingly.

sections.heading: Temperature (°C)
sections.body:
Ambient temperature near the plant. The sensor transmits this as an integer multiplied by 100 (e.g., 2350 = 23.50°C), which the app converts automatically.

  • Below 15°C — Too cold for most tropical houseplants.
  • 15–30°C — The default comfortable range.
  • Above 30°C — Heat stress risk, especially with direct sun on leaves.

The rule engine fires a critical alert if temperature is more than 5°C outside your plant's range.

sections.heading: Light (lux)
sections.body:
Light intensity measured in lux. This helps you know if your plant is getting enough (or too much) light.

  • Under 1,000 lux — Low light (labeled "low" in the app)
  • 1,000–2,000 lux — Medium light
  • Over 2,000 lux — High/bright light

If your plant's light preference is set to "high" but it's only getting 500 lux, the rule engine will suggest moving it to a brighter spot.

sections.heading: Battery (%)
sections.body: The sensor's remaining battery charge. You'll get a warning at 15% and a critical alert at 5%. See Battery & Charging for details.

sections.heading: Health Points (HP)
sections.body: HP is a single 0–100 score that summarizes how close your plant's conditions are to its ideal range. It's calculated by measuring the distance of each reading from the midpoint of its threshold range, then averaging. 100 HP means perfect conditions; lower scores mean something needs attention. If no readings are available, HP defaults to 50.
